does: This paper investigates a failure mode in machine unlearning where standard aggregate metrics mask non-uniform performance degradation. The authors conduct a fine-grained, example-level analysis comparing predictions of unlearned models to those of fully retrained models after deletion. They discover localized collateral forgetting: a phenomenon where prediction discrepancies grow with geometric proximity to the forget set. Through mechanistic analysis, they identify that surrogate targets used during unlearning create inconsistencies with the local prediction structure induced by retraining, causing this inconsistency to propagate through shared representations to nearby examples. They validate this pattern across gradient-ascent and random-labeling unlearning methods with and without retain-set fine-tuning.
connects: This work extends the machine unlearning literature, which has focused on removing training example influence since recent foundational work on data deletion and machine unlearning. While prior work established methods like gradient ascent and data deletion strategies, the field has relied primarily on aggregate metrics for evaluation. This paper responds to the limitation of summary-level evaluation by drawing on fine-grained analysis approaches from adversarial robustness and representation learning. It connects to work on how models encode training data locally and how information propagates through neural network representations. The mechanistic investigation of surrogate targets relates to broader understanding of knowledge distillation and label smoothing effects in neural networks.
enables: This work opens directions for improving unlearning methods by addressing the spatial structure of forgetting. The proposed Local Teacher Distillation approach, which uses soft labels from retained data rather than random targets, could inspire further refinements to unlearning algorithms that respect local prediction geometry. The identification of localized collateral forgetting as a mechanism enables better evaluation protocols that detect spatially non-uniform failures invisible to aggregate metrics. Future work could explore how geometric proximity measures interact with different network architectures, develop theoretical bounds on collateral forgetting radius, and investigate whether other unlearning methods exhibit similar localized failure patterns. This analysis may also inform privacy-utility tradeoffs in machine unlearning by quantifying how deletion impacts neighboring examples.
